rows.title: Cole vs the Lunch Wizard
paragraphs: Cole and the duckling were at the moon base. Cole had been saving the cupcakes all morning. The duckling was extra excited.
No cupcakes anywhere. Cole went puzzled like a tiny detective. The lunch wizard hummed a song nobody had asked for.
The trail led to the duckling. Of course. The duckling had a single crumb of cupcakes on its face. "YOU?" said Cole. The duckling looked away politely. Somewhere in there, Cole sprinted for emphasis.

Cole looked straight at the lunch wizard and said one word: "booger cloud." The lunch wizard confessed immediately. To a different crime. Cole noted it for later. Everything in the room had picked up a faint neon tint. "sneezy-pants," went somebody. Nobody asked who.
Back home, Cole high-fived the duckling over the case being officially closed. They both started planning the next caper. Justice, snacks, then more justice.

rows.title: The Day the Cereal Vanished
paragraphs: At the tiny city, Cole set the cereal down for one second. The turtle watched. Cole turned to grab a napkin. Just one second.
No cereal anywhere. Cole went snacky like a tiny detective. The fairy hummed a song nobody had asked for.

Plot twist nobody saw coming except maybe the turtle: it was the turtle. The turtle had been the cereal thief the whole time. It was very sorry. There was a small tiptoed moment that nobody quite witnessed in full. Somebody could have sworn the air went a little rose gold. Just then a "pickle crown" cracked the silence. Nobody admitted to it.
Cole looked straight at the fairy and said one word: "pickle wizard." The fairy confessed immediately. To a different crime. Cole noted it for later.
Back home, Cole high-fived the turtle over the case being officially closed. They both started planning the next caper. Justice, snacks, then more justice.

rows.title: The Day Cole Saved the Show
paragraphs: There was going to be a show. Cole had been planning it for days. The stage was the bus. The co-star was the eagle. The crucial prop was a jar of buttons.
The jar of buttons made a noise it should not have been able to make. Then it tipped sideways. Then it kept tipping. It was still tipping. The audience leaned in. Cole thought fast.
So Cole improvised. "tiny piano!" Cole yelled. Then slid. Then yelled "tiny piano!" again, louder. The pillows were INTO IT now. Everything in the room had picked up a faint electric blue tint.

"tiny piano!" yelled Cole, pointing at the broken jar of buttons. The jar of buttons twitched. Twitched again. Then it did exactly what it had refused to do five minutes ago. Pillows lost it. Cole kept feeling wobbly about the whole thing. Somebody had brought grilled cheese. Nobody knew when. Nobody minded. A fairy watched the whole thing happen and offered no comment.
Curtain down. Cole high-fived the eagle. "noodle scarf?" said Cole. "noodle scarf!" said the eagle. The new catchphrase had landed.

The waffles had vanished. Cole got sleepy about it. The substitute teacher tried a slow shrug. The shrug arrived late and crooked.
Right then the fennec fox hiccuped. Out came a tiny crumb of waffles. Then another. Then a third. The fennec fox looked at the crumbs. The crumbs looked back. Everybody knew.

Cole looked straight at the substitute teacher and said one word: "noodle-jumper." The substitute teacher confessed immediately. To a different crime. Cole noted it for later. For exactly two seconds, Cole marched like it was a job. There was a burnt orange feeling to the moment that nobody really named. And one of them, very quietly, said "pickle crown."
That night, Cole curled up. The fennec fox curled up too. Somewhere in the dark, very softly, Cole said "noodle-jumper" one more time. The fennec fox said it back. Then sleep.

The hallway ghost appeared out of nowhere. "You? Today? Trying to invent a brand new dance?" said the hallway ghost. "Absolutely not." It folded its arms in a way that meant business.

Cole walked up to the hallway ghost in full legendary mode. Not asking permission. Not apologizing. Just legendary. The hallway ghost had not prepared for legendary. The hallway ghost took a small step back. Somewhere in there, Cole wobbled for emphasis. A faint rainbow glow hung over the scene by then.

"moon spoon!" said Cole. The hallway ghost heard it, made a small noise, and stepped aside in a way that looked rehearsed. Cole invented a brand new dance. The tiger applauded the hallway ghost, because manners. And one of them, very quietly, said "bubble wand." some cereal sat off to the side, mostly forgotten, definitely still part of the day.
That night, Cole thought about it one more time and quietly said "moon spoon" into the dark. The tiger half-heard it and twitched a little ear in agreement.

But the ghost was in the way. The ghost did not want Cole to make the grumpiest thing giggle. The ghost had its own ideas, and the ideas were terrible.

And then Cole did the most mint green thing imaginable: walked right past the ghost while pretending to be a mint green traffic cone. The ghost watched, confused. The plan was working. Cole cartwheeled one more time, just to make a point.

"burp bubble!" said Cole. The ghost heard it, made a small noise, and stepped aside in a way that looked rehearsed. Cole made the grumpiest thing giggle. The koala applauded the ghost, because manners. Throughout, Cole stayed suspicious. Steadily suspicious. Somebody had brought smoothie. Nobody knew when. Nobody minded.
Cole climbed into bed. The koala climbed in too. The day got its closing line: "foot soup," whispered Cole. The koala sneezed in approval.

The courtroom duck declared the suspicious casserole forbidden. Cole felt gloriously incorrect about this development. Possibly more gloriously incorrect than the courtroom duck had bargained for.
Cole lurched heroically sideways while holding the mascot head. This was a different move than the one the rule said no to. A different move! The rule said nothing back.

The courtroom duck squinted at the mascot head. The courtroom duck could not technically object. Cole was technically still inside the rule. The courtroom duck muttered something and looked away. Somebody could have sworn the air went a little recess orange. Later someone would swear they heard "gloop" in the rafters.
"zoinks!" said Cole. The melodramatic cat nodded. Three witnesses appeared from nowhere. They also nodded. The courtroom duck tried to recount. The recount confirmed the loophole.
Heading home, Cole explained the loophole to the melodramatic cat one more time. The melodramatic cat was nodding seriously, clearly planning to deploy it later. Knowledge: transferred.

rows.title: Cole vs the Overqualified Fish
paragraphs: At the nurse's office, Cole set the suspicious casserole down for one second. The imperious corgi watched. Cole turned to grab a napkin. Just one second.
The suspicious casserole had vanished. Cole felt quietly heroic about it, in a way that meant business. The overqualified fish noticed and tried to act normal. It was not working.

Cole spotted it: a tiny eerie periwinkle crumb on the floor. Then another. Then another. The crumbs were leading somewhere. They were not leading to the overqualified fish. Somewhere in there, Cole marched stubbornly for emphasis.

Cole turned slowly to the imperious corgi. The imperious corgi pretended to be very interested in a leaf. The imperious corgi had been holding that leaf the whole time. It was a fake leaf. "Caught," said Cole. And one of them, very quietly, said "blorp."
Cole looked straight at the overqualified fish and said one word: "damp toast." The overqualified fish confessed immediately. To a different crime. Cole noted it for later.
Back home, Cole high-fived the imperious corgi over the case being officially closed. They both started planning the next caper. Justice, snacks, then more justice.

rows.title: Cole's Big Show
paragraphs: There was going to be a show. Cole had been planning it for days. The stage was the snack bar. The co-star was the overconfident raccoon. The crucial prop was a invisible box.
Then everything went wrong. The invisible box broke. Actually broke. In half. Cole froze. The overconfident raccoon froze. The pillows watched.
So Cole improvised. "rubber duck!" Cole yelled. Then posed dramatically. Then yelled "rubber duck!" again, louder. The pillows were INTO IT now.
The philosophical crab heckled. Genuinely heckled. The overconfident raccoon hissed back. Cole kept performing. Conflict made the bit better. "noodle scarf," went somebody. Nobody asked who.

Cole pointed at the broken invisible box and shouted "rubber duck." The invisible box reassembled itself in roughly the wrong order. The overconfident raccoon applauded the wrong order. The show was now better. For a beat the whole place looked weirdly forgotten gray. Throughout, Cole stayed surprisingly enthusiastic. Steadily surprisingly enthusiastic. some suspicious sandwiches sat off to the side, mostly forgotten, definitely still part of the day.
Backstage, Cole high-fived the overconfident raccoon. The show was unrepeatable, which felt right. Time to pack up and find the next thing.

rows.title: Cole and the Retired Dragon Take the Stage
paragraphs: At the kitchen, Cole set everything up. The retired dragon practiced its part. The apology balloon sat front and center. The whole show kind of depended on the apology balloon working.

The apology balloon just... gave up. It sat there. Completely uncooperative. The retired dragon poked it. Nothing. The retired dragon poked it again. Still nothing. Cole had eight seconds to fix this.

Cole took a breath, hovered suspiciously forward, and shouted whatever came to mind: "zoinks!" The audience leaned in. Sometimes nonsense lands. Throughout, Cole stayed surprisingly enthusiastic. Steadily surprisingly enthusiastic.

The suspicious accountant heckled. Genuinely heckled. The retired dragon hissed back. Cole kept performing. Conflict made the bit better. Cole noticed a streak of faded amber across the wall. Or did they.

"zoinks!" yelled Cole, pointing at the broken apology balloon. The apology balloon twitched. Twitched again. Then it did exactly what it had refused to do five minutes ago. Pillows lost it. "banana phone," went somebody. Nobody asked who. Somebody had brought courtroom cupcake. Nobody knew when. Nobody minded.
Backstage, Cole high-fived the retired dragon. The show was unrepeatable, which felt right. Time to pack up and find the next thing.

rows.title: Cole vs the Rule
paragraphs: It was a normal day at the noodle planet. Cole and the theatrical moth were minding their own business. The day was, technically, fine. So far.
The snack inspector declared the emergency burrito forbidden. Cole felt gloriously incorrect about this development. Possibly more gloriously incorrect than the snack inspector had bargained for.
Cole moonwalked carefully sideways while holding the foam finger. This was a different move than the one the rule said no to. A different move! The rule said nothing back.

The snack inspector tried to amend the rule on the fly. The snack inspector said, "Actually—" Cole held the foam finger higher. The snack inspector could not actually argue against that. The amendment was abandoned.

Cole held the emergency burrito up like a trophy. The snack inspector could only watch. "damp toast!" yelled Cole. Both things were now true at once. A faint luminous teal glow hung over the scene by then.
In bed, Cole thought about the loophole and quietly said "splat" one more time, just to seal it. The theatrical moth said "splat" too, like a co-signature. Bedtime: earned.

rows.title: Cole vs the Mysterious Substitute Teacher
paragraphs: At the farm, Cole had committed to one thing: the gas station nachos. The capybara was technically the witness. The mission, technically, involved the gas station nachos.
The gas station nachos had vanished. Cole felt terminally curious about it, in a way that meant business. The mysterious substitute teacher noticed and tried to act normal. It was not working.

Cole gracefully bailed past the mysterious substitute teacher in a way that read as deliberate. It was not deliberate. It happened to be exactly right. There was a burnt orange feeling to the moment that nobody really named.
The real culprit, obviously, was the capybara. Cole should have known. The capybara had crumbs of gas station nachos on its face and zero regret in its eyes.
Cole called out "wibble-whap" once, gently, like a summoning. The gas station nachos reappeared on the counter. Nobody saw who put it there. The capybara looked elsewhere very deliberately.
Walking home, Cole mentally workshopped how to retell this. The capybara was still chewing. "crusty pretzel," said Cole one last time, just to close the bit. The capybara said it back, mouth full.

rows.title: The Loophole at the Zoo
paragraphs: Cole was at the zoo, doing nothing in particular. The sleepy croc was nearby, also doing nothing. Things were going fine. They would not stay fine.

The aggressively normal pigeon declared the leftover pasta forbidden. Cole felt barely functional about this development. Possibly more barely functional than the aggressively normal pigeon had bargained for.

Cole located the loophole. It involved the lunch tray and a very specific reading of the rule. Cole did not point this out. Pointing it out is rookie behavior. There was a small chaotically bolted moment that nobody quite witnessed in full. A "crinkle-bonk" slipped out before Cole could stop it. And one of them, very quietly, said "thwip."
The aggressively normal pigeon watched Cole hold the lunch tray and felt outmaneuvered by precisely one inch of vocabulary. Bureaucracy is just words. Cole had the right ones.

It worked. Cole won. The rule was still there. The leftover pasta had also, somehow, ended up in Cole's hands. The aggressively normal pigeon retired for the day. Everything in the room had picked up a faint midnight blue tint.
On the walk home, Cole mentally filed the loophole for future use. The sleepy croc was definitely going to use it on Cole eventually. That was fair.

Cole walked off with the gas station nachos. The very confident rat could not technically argue. Cole filed it under: small wins, large vibes. Justice was unevenly served. As expected. Somewhere in there, Cole gracefully bailed for emphasis.

On the walk home, Cole filed the loophole for future use. The tiny possum was definitely going to use it back on Cole eventually. "sproing," said Cole one more time, quietly. The tiny possum saved it.
